coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria completeness and testability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and testability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default value of `src_dir` (e.g. `src/rebar/`) explicitly in the Files section so every executor targets the same tree.
evidence: 'lizard <src_dir>' and 'jscpd … <src_dir>' appear without a stated default value for src_dir
The plan references 'src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py' but never says src_dir defaults to 'src/rebar/' or 'src/'
findings.finding: The `src_dir` argument passed to both `lizard` and `jscpd` is never concretely defaulted in the plan. The script description says 'measure the tree' and references `src/rebar/` in one place, but never canonically sets the default target directory, leaving the executor to guess.
findings.location: Files section — 'Complexity tool + parse' and 'Clone tool + parse' bullets
scenarios: Executor points lizard at repo root and gets metrics for test files and vendored code, inflating CCN; another executor points at src/rebar/ — results differ and are not reproducible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line such as: 'Default `src_dir` is `src/rebar/`; overridable via CLI arg `--src-dir`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the git checkout strategy (e.g. `git worktree add <tmpdir> <commit>` per sampled commit, cleaned up after measurement) to prevent in-place checkout of the live working tree.
evidence: ABSENCE: no mention of git checkout strategy, worktree, or temp clone anywhere in the plan
The plan only says 'checking out sampled historical commits' without specifying the mechanism
findings.finding: The plan never specifies how the script checks out historical commits — in-place `git checkout`, `git worktree add`, or a bare clone. An in-place checkout in a dirty working tree clobbers unsaved work and may corrupt the repo state; a worktree approach avoids this but is meaningfully different to implement.
findings.location: Files section — `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py` description / Approach section
scenarios: Executor uses `git checkout` in-place on the live repo; if the script errors mid-run the working tree is left at a historical commit. Alternatively, executor uses git worktree, which is the safe approach but requires different implementation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files section: 'Checkout strategy: use `git worktree add <tmpdir> <commit>` for each sampled commit and remove the worktree after measurement; never mutate the main working tree.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion that runs the script against the actual repository (or a small real git fixture with real tool invocations in CI) and verifies at least one real snapshot appears in the store via `read_snapshots`, confirming the live execution path works end-to-end.
evidence: AC 1: 'with measure_tree stubbed, sampling 3 fixture commits writes 3 snapshots'
AC 2: 'a sampled commit whose measure_tree returns None … is skipped'
AC 3: 'every written snapshot carries source="snapshot"' — all asserted in test tier only
The stated end-state in What section: 'those two trends have history' — never verified by any AC
findings.finding: All three acceptance criteria are satisfied by stub-only pytest tests; no criterion verifies that the script actually runs against a real git repo and writes real snapshots to the going-forward store. The stated end-state ('those two trends have history') requires a successful live execution, but the only passing bar is green mocked tests.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all three ACs; Approach section ('This is an optional backfill script')
scenarios: Tests pass (stubs work), but the actual script fails silently on the real repo due to a jscpd flag incompatibility or a snapshot.py schema mismatch — the trends still have no real history but done is declared.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Running `python scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py --max-commits 1` against the project repo (with lizard and jscpd installed in CI) completes without error and `read_snapshots()` returns at least one snapshot with integer `complexity` and `clone_count` fields.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that verifies the sampling cadence is a CLI argument with a default of 'monthly' — e.g., assert that passing `--cadence weekly` to a fixture repo with ≥2 commits at least 1 week apart produces more than one snapshot (or assert the arg is accepted without error).
evidence: Files section: 'Sampling cadence is a script arg (default monthly)'
Approach section: 'Sampling cadence is a script arg (default monthly) — a stated default'
Absence: no AC line references the sampling cadence arg, its default, or its effect on output
findings.finding: The sampling cadence CLI argument (default monthly) is described in both the Files section and the Approach section as a deliverable feature of the script, but no Acceptance Criterion covers it — an implementer could omit the arg entirely without failing any AC.
findings.location: Files section ('Sampling cadence is a script arg (default monthly)'); Approach section ('Sampling cadence is a script arg (default monthly) — a stated default'); Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)
scenarios: An implementer hard-codes monthly sampling without exposing it as an arg; all three ACs pass but the described deliverable is absent.
A reviewer running the ACs cannot detect the missing CLI arg because no criterion exercises it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '- [ ] The backfill script accepts a `--cadence` (or equivalent) CLI argument defaulting to `monthly`; passing `--cadence weekly` to a fixture with commits spanning multiple weeks produces a different (larger) sample set than `--cadence monthly` — or at minimum the arg is accepted and forwarded to the sampler without error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard so the backfill skips (or overwrites) any commit whose snapshot already exists — e.g., check for an existing snapshot with matching `ts` and `source='snapshot'` before writing, or track processed commit SHAs in a side-file.
evidence: 'measure the tree and append snapshots via src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py'
No mention of deduplication, commit-hash tracking, or 'skip if snapshot for this ts already exists' logic anywhere in the plan or ACs.
findings.finding: The backfill script appends snapshots via snapshot.py with no idempotency guard; running it a second time (or re-running after a partial failure) would write duplicate snapshots for already-processed commits, corrupting trend analysis.
findings.location: Files section — `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`; Approach section: 'append snapshots via snapshot.py'
scenarios: Operator re-runs the backfill after a mid-run failure to recover skipped commits — all previously succeeded commits get a second snapshot.
Backfill is run again after adding new sampled commits — existing commits are written again.
Trend queries return doubled values for backfilled dates, making complexity/clone graphs misleading.
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling write_snapshot for a commit, call read_snapshots and check whether a snapshot with the same ts (commit date) and source='snapshot' already exists for that metric; if so, log and skip. Alternatively, pass the commit SHA as a snapshot field and deduplicate on that.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-time empirical probe (e.g., a developer-run `jscpd --reporters json --output /tmp/probe <src_dir>` with the actual project version and inspect the JSON) to validate the `statistics.total.clones` field path, and document the confirmed jscpd version/schema in a comment in measure_tree.
evidence: 'read <tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json and record clone_count = statistics.total.clones'
'the test monkeypatches THIS function to return stub values, so the test needs neither lizard nor jscpd installed'
No spike, fixture, or probe that runs real jscpd and verifies the JSON schema is present anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The jscpd JSON field path `statistics.total.clones` is asserted without empirical validation, and the test stubs measure_tree entirely, so no test or probe ever exercises the real jscpd output schema — a version mismatch would silently cause every clone-count measurement to fail (KeyError → None → commits skipped).
findings.location: Files section — clone tool parse: '`statistics.total.clones`'; Tests section — 'measure_tree stubbed'
scenarios: jscpd version in the project's environment uses a different top-level key (e.g., `duplicates` instead of `statistics.total.clones` — the schema has changed across major versions).
Field path is nested differently in an older/newer jscpd release; KeyError is caught, measure_tree returns None, all clone-count snapshots are silently absent with only a log line.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a comment in measure_tree citing the jscpd version (e.g., '# validated against jscpd 3.x; field path: statistics.total.clones') and add a lightweight integration smoke-test (marked skip-in-CI) that runs real jscpd on a two-file fixture and asserts the field exists — this can live alongside the unit test and be gated on jscpd being installed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify idempotency semantics for `write_snapshot` when called with a `ts` that already exists (e.g., upsert/skip-if-present), or add a pre-check in the backfill script that skips commits whose snapshot already exists in the store, so re-runs are safe.
evidence: 'for sampled commits (default monthly), measure the tree and append snapshots via src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py'
'write_snapshot(record, ts=<commit ISO date>, repo_root=...)'
No mention of upsert semantics, deduplication by ts, or a 'skip if already present' check anywhere in the plan.
ABSENCE: no idempotency/at-least-once behaviour specified in either the script design or the snapshot.py writer contract.
findings.finding: The plan describes appending snapshots with no idempotency or deduplication guarantee. Re-running the backfill (e.g., after a mid-run failure or operator mistake) will write a second set of snapshots for the same commits at the same ts values, duplicating every data point and corrupting trend calculations (double-counted complexity/clone totals).
findings.location: Files section — 'measure_tree(tree_path)' + 'write_snapshot(record, ts=<commit ISO date>, ...)'; Approach section — 'append snapshots via snapshot.py'
scenarios: A backfill run fails at commit 7 of 12; operator re-runs the script; commits 1–6 receive a second snapshot each, doubling their complexity/clone values in the trend series and producing misleading charts.
An automated CI job runs the backfill twice (e.g., due to a retry policy); every historical data point is duplicated silently with no error raised.
findings.suggested_fix: Before calling write_snapshot for a commit, query read_snapshots for an existing entry with the same ts (and metric type). If one exists, log 'already backfilled, skipping' and continue. Alternatively, specify that write_snapshot accepts an `overwrite=False` flag that makes it a no-op when the ts is already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add AC or script-design language covering: (a) the empty-sample case exits with an informative message (e.g. 'No commits matched the sampling criteria'), and (b) a write_snapshot failure for one commit is logged and counted but does not abort the run, with a final summary line ('N of M commits backfilled').
evidence: AC covers: 3-commit happy path, tool-absent skip, source/confidence tags — no AC for zero commits sampled or for write_snapshot raising an exception.
ABSENCE: no mention of exit code, summary output, or error message when the backfill writes zero snapshots (empty result) or when the store write fails.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no handling for the case where zero commits are sampled (e.g., the repo has fewer commits than the sampling interval, or the date range yields no results), nor for a write_snapshot failure mid-run, leaving the operator with no signal that the backfill produced nothing or terminated early.
findings.location: Tests section — 'Unit (temp git repo + stubbed tool output)'; Acceptance Criteria — all three ACs cover successful writes or tool-failure skips, but not empty-sample or store-write-failure cases.
scenarios: Operator runs the backfill on a shallow clone or a date-range with no matching commits; the script exits silently with zero snapshots written and no indication that the backfill was a no-op.

A read-only or full disk causes write_snapshot to raise an exception mid-run; the script aborts (or continues silently) leaving a partial backfill with no summary of how many commits succeeded vs. failed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace direct git checkout of the main working tree with an isolated approach — either `git worktree add <tmpdir> <commit>` (leaves the main tree intact) or a fresh shallow clone into a temp directory — so the backfill does not mutate the live checkout and is safe in CI and remote/read-only deployments.
evidence: 'checkout + tool run at each sampled commit'
'git show -s --format=%cI <commit>' implies git operations are run against the current checkout
ABSENCE: no mention of git worktree, temp clone, or bare-repo approach to isolate historical checkouts from the live working tree.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the script to check out sampled historical commits in the main working tree ('checkout + tool run at each sampled commit'). This mutates the live working tree, which breaks CI environments with concurrent jobs sharing the same checkout, and fails entirely in server/remote-MCP deployments where the working tree is read-only or not locally present.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Complexity/clone are point-in-time … which git makes deterministic (checkout + tool run at each sampled commit)'
scenarios: CI (platform: CI, project location: in-checkout): a parallel CI job (e.g., the going-forward metric collection) is running against the same checkout while the backfill checks out an old commit; the parallel job reads a tree from a different point in history, producing wrong metric values or a git 'detached HEAD' conflict error.

Remote MCP server (harness: remote MCP, project location: server outside the checkout): the rebar server has read-only access to the target repo path; git checkout fails with a permission error, and the backfill script exits with an unhandled exception rather than a clean error message.
Interrupted run (any venue): if the script is killed mid-checkout, the working tree is left in a detached HEAD state, breaking all subsequent git operations for the developer or CI pipeline.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `git worktree add --detach <tmpdir> <commit_sha>` for each sampled commit, run the tools against `<tmpdir>`, then `git worktree remove <tmpdir>`. This keeps the main working tree clean and is safe for concurrent use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'checking out sampled historical commits' with the existing safe tree-materialization path (e.g. `git archive <sha> | tar -x` via `src/rebar/llm/workflow/snapshot.py`, or `git read-tree + checkout-index` pattern from ADR 0005) so the backfill script never mutates the live worktree.
evidence: Plan: 'by checking out sampled historical commits, running the metric tools'
ADR 0005 (docs/adr/0005-snapshot-cache-architecture.md line 36): 'Rejected alternative: `git worktree add` per ref — takes repo-level index/config locks (the exact contention we avoid)'
src/rebar/llm/workflow/snapshot.py: git-archive-based commit snapshot already in production use
src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py: read-tree+checkout-index materialization also present
findings.finding: The plan proposes 'checking out sampled historical commits' to materialize trees for tool runs, but the codebase already has two safe, lock-free, live-worktree-preserving tree-materialization paths: `src/rebar/llm/workflow/snapshot.py` (git archive) and `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` (git read-tree + checkout-index per ADR 0005). A naive `git checkout` of the live worktree is explicitly the approach ADR 0005 rejected ('Rejected alternative: `git worktree add` per ref — takes repo-level index/config locks'). The plan rebuilds tree materialization with a more dangerous mechanism than what already exists.
findings.location: Files section — `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py` / Approach section: 'checking out sampled historical commits'
scenarios: Running the backfill script while the MCP server or another job has the worktree checked out at a different commit causes `git checkout` to mutate the shared working tree mid-operation.
A crash or interrupt during `git checkout` leaves the repo in a detached-HEAD or dirty state.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `src/rebar/llm/workflow/snapshot.py`'s `build_snapshot(sha, repo_root=...)` to materialize each sampled commit into a temp directory, then pass that path as `tree_path` to `measure_tree`. This reuses the existing safe path and avoids any live-worktree mutation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `lizard` or `radon` should be the single complexity tool for both backfill snapshots and ongoing measurement, and document the choice; if `radon` is already the project standard (per the maintenance runbook), prefer it over introducing a second complexity scanner.
evidence: docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 22: '| `radon` | 6.0.1 | `.venv/bin/pip install radon` | Cyclomatic complexity (`cc`) + maintainability index (`mi`) |'
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 50: '`.venv/bin/radon cc src/rebar -s -n C --total-average > "$OUT/radon_cc.txt"'

src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py line 337: lizard usage is only a presence-check abstain path with 'the rich per-function complexity path is a follow-up' — no existing complexity-extraction logic to reuse
Plan: 'cyclomatic complexity; already a project dependency-of-record for grounding'
findings.finding: The plan uses `lizard` for cyclomatic complexity, but `radon cc` is already the documented, confirmed-available cyclomatic complexity tool for this codebase (listed in `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md` as the complexity scanner). Introducing `lizard` for the same metric in the backfill path creates a second complexity-measurement mechanism without justification, while `radon` already produces per-function CCN with `--json` output and is documented as available.
findings.location: Files section — Complexity tool + parse bullet: '`lizard <src_dir>` (cyclomatic complexity)'
scenarios: Two tools (`lizard` in backfill, `radon` in manual audit) produce different CCN totals for the same commit due to parser differences, making historical and current snapshots inconsistent.
A future maintainer adding going-forward complexity snapshots picks `radon` (the documented tool) and creates irreconcilable series.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'already a project dependency-of-record' claim for lizard: it is an external CLI (not a pip dep) and must be documented as a prerequisite the operator must install, with explicit setup instructions or a check in the script.
evidence: pyproject.toml: no 'lizard' entry in [project.dependencies] or any optional-dependencies block
pyproject.toml line 142: 'The out-of-process backends (universal-ctags, OpenGrep/ast-grep, scc/lizard) are external CLIs, not pip deps.'
engine_b_backends.py line 337: 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here, so this fails open.'
findings.finding: The plan asserts lizard is 'already a project dependency-of-record' (citing src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py), but pyproject.toml contains no lizard in any dependency section; pyproject.toml line 142 explicitly classifies scc/lizard as 'external CLIs, not pip deps,' and engine_b_backends.py itself comments that 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here, so this fails open.' The 'dependency-of-record' characterization is false.
findings.location: Files section — 'Complexity tool + parse' bullet: 'already a project dependency-of-record for grounding, see src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py'
scenarios: A developer reads the plan, trusts that lizard is a declared project dep, and skips installing it before running the backfill script — the script silently skips every commit instead of measuring complexity.
CI environment does not have lizard on PATH; all complexity snapshots are skipped (None returned) with no visible error, producing a silently empty backfill.
findings.suggested_fix: Change the plan's description to: 'lizard is an external CLI prerequisite (not a pip dep — see pyproject.toml). Document that the operator must install it (e.g. pip install lizard) before running the backfill, or add a preflight check in measure_tree that surfaces a clear error when lizard is absent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact jscpd JSON output filename and the `statistics.total.clones` field path by running `npx jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src>` against a test directory and reading the actual report structure, then update the plan with the verified path.
evidence: docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md line 65: 'npx jscpd src/rebar --min-tokens 50 --min-lines 8 --format python --reporters json --output "$OUT/jscpd"' — output is a directory, not a file
No file in the repo parses jscpd JSON output or references 'jscpd-report.json' or 'statistics.total.clones'
ABSENCE: zero grep hits for 'jscpd-report.json' or 'statistics.total.clones' anywhere in the codebase
findings.finding: The plan asserts `jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>` produces `<tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json` (the exact file path the script reads), but no existing code or documentation in the repo parses jscpd JSON output or references `jscpd-report.json` as a filename. The maintenance-audit-runbook uses `--output "$OUT/jscpd"` (a directory), and the actual output filename under that directory is unverified by any repo artifact.
findings.location: Files section — 'Clone tool + parse' bullet: 'jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>; read <tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json and record clone_count = statistics.total.clones'
scenarios: The actual jscpd output filename may differ (e.g. 'jscpd-report.json' vs a subdirectory layout), causing the script to fail with a FileNotFoundError on the report read.

The JSON field path 'statistics.total.clones' may not match jscpd's actual schema (e.g. it could be 'statistics.clones.total' or a different nesting), causing a silent KeyError that returns None and skips the commit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC1 to specify a valid `ts`-verification method: either (a) instruct the test to read the raw `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` lines and parse the outer envelope, or (b) note that `ts` must be embedded inside the record dict passed to `write_snapshot` so `read_snapshots` returns it.
evidence: snapshot.py line 73: `out.append(entry.get('record', {}))` — only the inner record is returned, not the outer envelope containing `ts`
AC1 quote: 'asserted by reading the snapshots back via `read_snapshots` and checking the per-commit `ts`'
The `ts` key lives in the outer NDJSON envelope `{"ts": ..., "record": {...}}` which `read_snapshots` deliberately strips
findings.finding: `read_snapshots()` strips the outer `{"ts": ..., "record": ...}` envelope and returns only the inner record dict — so AC1's instruction to assert per-commit `ts` values 'by reading the snapshots back via `read_snapshots`' is unimplementable: `ts` will not be present in the returned dicts.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: '…asserted by reading the snapshots back via `read_snapshots` and checking the per-commit `ts` and int-typed values'
scenarios: A test author faithfully follows AC1 and asserts `rec['ts'] == commit_date` on results from `read_snapshots` — the assertion KeyErrors on every record, making the test fail or require a workaround the plan never mentions.
The test instead puts `ts` inside the `record` dict to make the AC pass — which deviates from how `write_snapshot` is called in the going-forward path and corrupts the snapshot format.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC1 to: '…asserted by reading `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` directly and parsing each line's outer `ts` field, OR by embedding `ts` in the record dict so `read_snapshots` returns it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground `jscpd` as a project dependency: either cite where it is already declared (pyproject.toml, package.json, CI install step) or add a note that it must be added as a new dependency alongside this script.
evidence: grep for 'jscpd' across all of src/ returns zero matches
lizard is grounded: 'src/rebar/grounding/engine_b_backends.py' references it explicitly
jscpd is given no equivalent grounding citation in the plan
findings.finding: `jscpd` has zero presence in the codebase (unlike `lizard`, which is cited in `engine_b_backends.py`). The plan presents it as a tool-of-record without any grounding citation, making it an unvalidated new external dependency.
findings.location: Files section — 'Clone tool + parse: `jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>`'
scenarios: jscpd is not installed in the project environment or CI, and the backfill script silently skips all clone measurements (treating it as a tool error), producing a series with no clone_count data.
The jscpd JSON output format (`statistics.total.clones`) differs across versions, causing silent parse failures.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the implied `git checkout <commit>` mechanism with `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py`'s `_materialize_tree()` (or `git read-tree` + `git checkout-index --prefix=<tmpdir>/` with a throwaway `GIT_INDEX_FILE`) so each commit is materialized into a temp directory without touching the live working tree.
evidence: Plan says: 'One-time backfill of the complexity and clone-count series by checking out sampled historical commits'
src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py:_materialize_tree() — uses GIT_INDEX_FILE throwaway to materialize any SHA to a temp dir without touching the live worktree or index.lock
repo_snapshot.py docstring: 'neither read-tree nor checkout-index touches the repo's own index.lock/config.lock or working tree, so two materializations of different SHAs never contend'
A git checkout mid-run failure leaves the working tree at the wrong historical commit — requiring a manual git checkout HEAD restore to recover
findings.finding: The plan's core tree-materialization mechanism ('checking out sampled historical commits') implies git checkout on the live working tree, which is destructive: it mutates the shared working tree, risks losing uncommitted changes, and if the script errors mid-run leaves the repo stuck at a historical commit. The repo already has src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py:_materialize_tree() which uses git read-tree + git checkout-index with a throwaway GIT_INDEX_FILE — explicitly designed to materialize any SHA into a temp directory without touching the working tree or taking the index lock. This is a clearly-superior, already-available alternative.
findings.location: ## Approach / ## Files — backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py description ('checking out sampled historical commits')
scenarios: Script fails between commits — working tree is left at an old historical commit; any subsequent git operations (CI, other scripts) operate on the wrong tree state.
Script is run while uncommitted changes are present — git checkout on a historical commit drops or stashes those changes.
Two concurrent runs of the backfill would race on the shared working tree.
findings.suggested_fix: For each sampled commit SHA, call the existing `_materialize_tree(repo_root, sha, dest_tmp)` from `src/rebar/_snapshot/repo_snapshot.py` (or replicate its `git read-tree <sha> && git checkout-index --all --prefix=<tmpdir>/` pattern with `GIT_INDEX_FILE` set to a throwaway path). Pass `dest_tmp` as the `tree_path` argument to `measure_tree()`. This is lock-free, worktree-safe, and already tested by the repo.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard: before writing a snapshot for a sampled commit, check whether a record with the same commit SHA (stored in the record, e.g. as a `commit` key) already exists in the snapshot file, and skip the write if so — mirroring the `_existing_ticket_ids` pattern in `scripts/backfill_bug_class.py`.
evidence: snapshot.py line 46: `with path.open('a', encoding='utf-8') as fh: fh.write(line + '\n')` — pure append, no dedup
Plan says 'append snapshots via snapshot.py' with no mention of a guard on already-processed commits
ABSENCE: no mention of tracking which commits have already been written (e.g., by commit SHA in the record or a separate seen-set check before writing)
findings.finding: The plan specifies no idempotency guard on the backfill writes. `write_snapshot()` is a pure append (confirmed: `path.open('a')`), so re-running the script — after a partial failure, operator mistake, or CI retry — silently appends duplicate snapshot records for already-processed commits, corrupting the trend series with doubled data points.
findings.location: ## Files — backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py: 'append snapshots via snapshot.py' / ## Approach
scenarios: Script interrupted after 5 of 20 commits; re-run writes all 20, giving duplicates for the first 5.
Operator runs the script twice on the same repo; all commits are doubled in the snapshot store.
findings.suggested_fix: Include the commit SHA as a field in each written record (e.g. `{"commit": sha, "complexity": ..., "clone_count": ...}`). Before the loop (or per-commit), read existing snapshots and build a `seen_commits` set; skip `write_snapshot` for any commit already present.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale in the Approach section explaining the tree-materialization mechanism choice (e.g., why `git read-tree`+`checkout-index` into a tmpdir, or the `_materialize_tree()` seam from `repo_snapshot.py`, is used instead of `git checkout` on the live working tree).
evidence: Plan states the approach goal ('checking out sampled historical commits') but provides no rationale for the materialization mechanism

The plan does justify the snapshot.py writer choice ('writes through the same snapshot.py writer ... so backfilled and live snapshots share one format') but leaves the tree-checkout mechanism unspecified
ABSENCE: no statement of why the chosen checkout approach is safe for the shared working tree
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for the tree-materialization mechanism choice (what specific git operation is used to check out historical commits and why it is safe for a one-time backfill script). The 'why this fits' for the checkout step is entirely absent, leaving the implementer to choose freely between `git checkout`, `git worktree add`, `git archive`, or the repo's existing `_materialize_tree()` plumbing.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'by checking out sampled historical commits'
scenarios: Implementer defaults to `git checkout` on the live working tree (the most obvious reading) and introduces the working-tree-mutation hazard described in finding 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the leaf's snapshot labels to the parent's mandated vocabulary: change `source="snapshot"` → `source="backfill_classified"` and `confidence="high"` → `confidence="classified"` in both the Approach section and AC3, and confirm `is_authoritative()` returns False for these records.
evidence: Leaf Approach: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high` (deterministic tool output)'
Leaf AC3: 'every written snapshot carries `source="snapshot"`, `confidence="high"` — asserted'

Parent Success Criteria: 'every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`), and `is_authoritative()` returns False for it so it is never aggregated into an authoritative structural series'
Parent AC5: 'at least one backfilled `caused_by`/`close_class`/snapshot value exists carrying `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`'

The leaf's `confidence=high` label would not trigger `is_authoritative()=False` under the parent's contract, causing backfilled snapshots to be aggregated into the authoritative structural series — the exact outcome the parent's Success Criteria forbids.
findings.finding: The leaf mandates `source="snapshot"` and `confidence="high"` on every backfilled snapshot, directly contradicting the parent's explicit, exclusive backfill vocabulary of `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified`. The parent further states `is_authoritative()` must return False for all backfilled values and that 'there is no `backfill_structural`' — meaning `confidence=high` would cause these records to be treated as authoritative and aggregated into the structural series, violating the parent's Success Criteria and AC.
findings.location: Leaf: Approach section ('snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`') and AC3 ('every written snapshot carries `source="snapshot"`, `confidence="high"`'); Parent: Success Criteria ('every backfilled/classified value is labeled `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (the single backfill vocabulary — there is no `backfill_structural`), and `is_authoritative()` returns False') and AC5 ('at least one backfilled…snapshot value exists carrying `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`').
scenarios: The `rebar metrics` command aggregates snapshots where `is_authoritative()` is True; a `confidence=high` backfilled snapshot enters the authoritative series and corrupts trend calculations with artificially seeded historical data.
The parent AC5 integration test asserts `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified` on snapshot values — the leaf's AC3 asserts the opposite, so both tests cannot pass simultaneously.
findings.suggested_fix: In the leaf's Approach section, replace 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`' with 'snapshots are labeled `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`' (the parent's single backfill vocabulary). Update AC3 to assert `source="backfill_classified"` and `confidence="classified"`. This realigns the leaf to be a strict subset of the parent's Success Criteria and AC5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and document the exact `lizard` CLI invocation and JSON output field path used to extract per-function CCN (e.g., `lizard --csv` or `lizard -l python -E ...`), and confirm it differs from the scc-only parse path already in `engine_b_backends.py`; alternatively, justify using `radon cc` (the complexity tool already documented in `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md`) instead.
evidence: engine_b_backends.py:337: 'Metric matcher. scc/lizard are NOT installed here, so this fails open.'
engine_b_backends.py:370-412: _metric_invoke only invokes the binary with '--format json' and reads 'Code' (LOC) — no CCN parsing anywhere in the codebase
engine_b.py:60: `_METRIC_CANDIDATES = ('scc', 'lizard')` — lizard is a fallback candidate, but real parsing is scc-only
The plan claims: 'Parse its per-function CCN and record complexity = the SUMMED cyclomatic complexity' — this parse path is absent from the codebase
findings.finding: The plan asserts `lizard <src_dir>` is the correct tool for cyclomatic complexity and that its per-function CCN output can be parsed and summed — but the existing codebase invocation (`engine_b_backends.py:_metric_invoke`) only ever parses scc's `Code` (LOC) field, never lizard's CCN output. The plan calls lizard a 'dependency-of-record' yet the actual implemented path has never exercised lizard's CCN parsing. This specific capability (produce parseable per-function CCN via `lizard <path>`) is unverified in the repo.
findings.location: Files section — 'Complexity tool + parse: lizard <src_dir> (cyclomatic complexity; already a project dependency-of-record…)'
scenarios: Implementer writes code assuming `lizard --json src/` or similar emits CCN in a known schema, but finds no verified schema in the codebase to copy from
The lizard CLI output format has changed across versions; without a verified invocation+parse example, the script may silently record 0 or error

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact jscpd output filename and the JSON field path for clone count against the installed/pinned jscpd version (run `npx jscpd --version` and inspect an actual output file), and document the verified field path (e.g., `statistics.total.clones` or `statistics.clones`) in the plan.
evidence: docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:65: `npx jscpd src/rebar --min-tokens 50 --min-lines 8 --format python --reporters json --output "$OUT/jscpd"` — runs jscpd but output is never read programmatically in this repo
examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/references/patterns.md:92: `npx jscpd --min-lines 10 --min-tokens 70 --reporters console .` — console reporter only
ABSENCE: no file in the repo reads jscpd JSON output or references `statistics.total.clones`
ABSENCE: jscpd's output file is named by jscpd itself; whether it is 'jscpd-report.json' under the --output dir depends on the jscpd version
findings.finding: The plan asserts `jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>` writes `<tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json` with a `statistics.total.clones` field. Neither the output filename `jscpd-report.json` nor the JSON schema path `statistics.total.clones` appears anywhere in the codebase's jscpd usages — the maintenance runbook runs jscpd but never reads its JSON output programmatically. This is an unverified capability assertion about jscpd's output contract.
findings.location: Files section — 'Clone tool + parse: jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>; read <tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json and record clone_count = statistics.total.clones'
scenarios: jscpd writes `report.json` or `jscpd-report.json` depending on version; the script hardcodes the wrong filename and silently skips every commit
`statistics.total.clones` is not the correct JSON path for clone count in the installed jscpd version, so clone_count is always None/0

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the choice of `lizard` over `radon cc` (already confirmed available in `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md`) for cyclomatic complexity measurement, or switch to `radon cc` and document why it is preferred for the backfill use-case.
evidence: docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:22: `radon | 6.0.1 | .venv/bin/pip install radon | Cyclomatic complexity (cc) + maintainability index (mi)` — confirmed available in the project
docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:50: `.venv/bin/radon cc src/rebar -s -n C --total-average` — verified working invocation
The plan states lizard is 'already a project dependency-of-record' but it is not in pyproject.toml [project.dependencies] or any extras; it is only a candidate in _METRIC_CANDIDATES alongside scc
findings.finding: The plan chooses `lizard` for cyclomatic complexity without considering `radon`, which is the tool the project's own `docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md` already documents and confirms available (version 6.0.1) for exactly this purpose (`radon cc` — cyclomatic complexity). Preferring lizard over the already-validated in-project tool introduces an unacknowledged dependency without justification.
findings.location: Files section — 'Complexity tool + parse: lizard <src_dir> (cyclomatic complexity; already a project dependency-of-record…)'
scenarios: lizard is not installed in the environment where the backfill script runs; radon (already documented as available) would have worked without additional setup
An implementer spends effort integrating lizard's CCN output format when radon cc's output is already understood by the project
findings.suggested_fix: Use `radon cc --json <src_dir>` (already project-documented, Python-native, parseable JSON output) instead of `lizard`; sum the `complexity` field across all functions in the JSON output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency guard to the backfill script (e.g., record processed commit SHAs in the snapshot record and skip any commit whose SHA already appears in the store, or write a sidecar progress file) so re-running after partial failure does not produce duplicate entries.
evidence: Plan: 'measure the tree and append snapshots via `src/rebar/metrics/snapshot.py`' — pure append, no dedup described
`write_snapshot` opens file with `path.open('a', ...)` — no uniqueness enforcement
`read_snapshots` returns ALL records in a timestamp range — multiple identical rows all returned
Plan's only failure handling: 'A tool that is absent or errors makes `measure_tree` return `None` and the commit is skipped' — addresses tool errors only, not script-level interruption and restart
findings.finding: The backfill script has no idempotency / deduplication guard: `write_snapshot` is a pure append and the plan describes no mechanism to skip commits that have already been written. If the script is interrupted mid-run and restarted, every commit processed before the interruption is written a second time, producing duplicate entries in `.rebar/metrics-snapshots.ndjson` that silently corrupt trend charts.
findings.location: Files section — `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`; snapshot.py `write_snapshot` (pure append, line 39-47)
scenarios: Operator runs the backfill; lizard/jscpd stalls on commit #8 of 24; operator kills the process and reruns — commits 1–7 are now doubled in the NDJSON store, inflating summed complexity values and double-counting clone blocks in every chart that reads those months.
CI runs the backfill as a one-off job that times out; the retry job re-processes the full range.
findings.suggested_fix: Before writing, call `read_snapshots` for the commit's date range and skip the commit if a record with `source='snapshot'` and a matching `commit_sha` key already exists. Alternatively, store a `.rebar/backfill-progress.json` sidecar that tracks processed SHAs and is updated atomically after each successful write.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document (or implement) a rollback path for the backfill — at minimum, state that the NDJSON file is git-tracked so `git revert`/`git checkout` can restore it, note that live snapshots written after the backfill would also be lost by a file-level revert, and add a `--dry-run` flag that prints what would be written without modifying the store.
evidence: Plan: 'Writes through the same `snapshot.py` writer as the going-forward path' — pure append, no delete/update API
snapshot.py has no `delete_snapshots` or `truncate_snapshots` surface
Plan never mentions git as a rollback mechanism for the NDJSON file despite snapshot.py docs stating it is git-tracked
ABSENCE: no 'dry-run' flag, no 'rollback' section, no 'preview before write' step described in the plan
findings.finding: The plan specifies no back-out / rollback path for bad backfill data. If the script writes incorrect metric values (e.g., lizard misconfigured, wrong `src_dir`), there is no described procedure to identify and remove those rows from the append-only NDJSON store. The plan never states that the git-tracked nature of the file enables `git revert` as a rollback, nor does it address the post-rollback schema state of the file.
findings.location: Approach section; Files section — `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
scenarios: Backfill runs with `src_dir` pointing at the wrong directory; all 24 monthly snapshots record complexity=0. Operator has no documented procedure to remove those rows without hand-editing the NDJSON file.
A second operator re-runs the script without knowing a prior run completed; duplicates exist and neither operator knows the canonical state of the file after partial rollback attempts.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `jscpd` should be invoked as `jscpd` (global binary) or `npx jscpd` (matching the runbook's established pattern), and update the plan's invocation accordingly.
evidence: Plan: `jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>`
Runbook (docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md:65): `npx jscpd src/rebar --min-tokens 50 --min-lines 8 --format python --reporters json --output "$OUT/jscpd"`
Partially-verified: jscpd exists as a tool but the specific invocation surface (bare binary vs npx) is unconfirmed
findings.finding: The plan invokes `jscpd` as a bare binary (`jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>`) but the only prior usage in the repo runs it via `npx jscpd` — indicating jscpd is a Node.js tool accessed through npx, not a standalone PATH binary. If the execution environment has Node/npx but not a globally installed `jscpd`, the bare invocation will silently fail and every commit will be skipped.
findings.location: Files section — Clone tool + parse: `jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>`
scenarios: CI environment has npx but no globally installed jscpd — every commit is silently skipped as a 'tool absent' rather than a misconfiguration error
Developer follows the plan and gets zero clone_count snapshots with no clear error message
findings.suggested_fix: Change plan invocation to `npx jscpd --reporters json --output <tmpdir> <src_dir>` to match the established runbook pattern, and note that Node.js/npx is a runtime precondition.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify `statistics.total.clones` against jscpd's actual JSON output schema (run `npx jscpd --reporters json` on a sample dir and inspect the output), and add the verified key path plus a sample fixture to the plan.
evidence: Plan: 'read `<tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json` and record `clone_count` = `statistics.total.clones` (the number of duplicated blocks, an `int`)'
ABSENCE: no jscpd output parser exists anywhere in src/ or tests/
ABSENCE: no jscpd sample JSON output file in the repo to verify the schema
Unverified: specific field path `statistics.total.clones` in jscpd's JSON output
findings.finding: The plan asserts the jscpd JSON output key path `statistics.total.clones` as the field containing the duplicate-block count, but no existing code in the repository parses jscpd JSON output and no jscpd sample output is referenced. This field path is entirely unverified against jscpd's actual JSON schema — a wrong key name would silently yield `None`/`KeyError` rather than a count.
findings.location: Files section — Clone tool + parse: `read <tmpdir>/jscpd-report.json` and record `clone_count` = `statistics.total.clones`
scenarios: Actual jscpd JSON uses a different key (e.g. `duplicates` or `statistics.duplicates`) — all snapshots record `clone_count=None` silently
jscpd version change alters output schema — historical backfill produces all-zero or all-null clone counts

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact `lizard` invocation flags needed for machine-readable output (e.g. `lizard --json <src_dir>` or `lizard -o json <src_dir>`) and confirm the JSON field path for per-function CCN values.
evidence: Plan: 'lizard <src_dir> (cyclomatic complexity; already a project dependency-of-record…). Parse its per-function CCN'
ABSENCE: no lizard invocation or output parser anywhere in src/ (engine_b_backends.py comment: 'scc/lizard are NOT installed here')
pyproject.toml:142: 'The out-of-process backends (universal-ctags, OpenGrep/ast-grep, scc/lizard) are external CLIs, not pip deps'
Partially-verified: lizard CLI exists and supports CCN, but the specific flag for structured output is unconfirmed
findings.finding: The plan calls `lizard <src_dir>` and describes parsing 'per-function CCN' to produce a summed integer, but does not specify a `--json` (or `-o json`) output flag. Lizard's default output is plain text; reliable structured parsing of CCN values requires invoking lizard with a machine-readable output format. No lizard output parser exists in the repository to confirm the parsing approach.
findings.location: Files section — Complexity tool + parse: `lizard <src_dir>` (cyclomatic complexity…)
scenarios: Implementer shells to `lizard src/` and gets plain-text output, writes a fragile regex parser that breaks on edge cases
Lizard version differences change text format — summed CCN silently miscalculates
findings.suggested_fix: Use `lizard --json <src_dir>` (lizard supports `--json` for structured output) and document the field path (e.g. `function_list[*].cyclomatic_complexity`) used to compute the summed integer.

findings.finding: The plan never specifies what ISO-8601 timestamp value is passed as `ts` to `write_snapshot` for each backfilled commit. Using wall-clock time (the time the backfill runs) instead of the commit's author/committer date would silently store all backfilled snapshots with today's timestamp, corrupting the historical time series the feature is meant to produce.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'For each sampled commit, `ts` is set to that commit's ISO-8601 committer date (obtained via `git log -1 --format=%cI <sha>`), so backfilled snapshots land at their true historical positions in the time series.'

findings.finding: The plan states 'scc/lizard (complexity)' as the tool pair for complexity metrics, but the existing codebase integration (`engine_b_backends.py`) uses scc only for LOC/oversize (the `Code` field), and explicitly defers per-function cyclomatic complexity ('the rich per-function complexity path is a follow-up'). Lizard is not yet integrated anywhere in the Python codebase. Meanwhile, the maintenance audit runbook uses `radon` (a Python library, already known to the project) for cyclomatic complexity. The plan's assertion that scc/lizard jointly produce a `complexity` snapshot key is unverified against the actual tool outputs and the repo's current integration state.
findings.location: Approach section: 'run scc/lizard (complexity)'; Files section description

findings.finding: The plan names 'complexity' and 'clone_count' as required snapshot record keys (AC1) but never defines their value schema: what type (int, float), what unit (total cyclomatic complexity? average? LOC?), or how scc/lizard/jscpd raw output is aggregated into a single scalar per commit. An implementer cannot write a correct parser without inventing this schema.
findings.location: Approach section ('snapshots labeled source=snapshot, confidence=high'); AC1 ('containing `complexity` and `clone_count` keys')

findings.finding: The leaf labels backfilled snapshots `source=snapshot, confidence=high`, directly contradicting the parent's explicit single backfill vocabulary (`source=backfill_classified`, `confidence=classified`) and the parent's AC5, which asserts that snapshot backfill values must carry exactly `source=backfill_classified` + `confidence=classified`.
findings.location: Leaf §Approach: 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`'; Parent §Success Criteria (backfill vocabulary bullet) and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: In §Approach, replace 'snapshots are labeled `source=snapshot, confidence=high`' with 'snapshots are labeled `source=backfill_classified, confidence=classified`'. Add to AC1: verify each snapshot carries `source=backfill_classified` and `confidence=classified` (e.g., `jq -e '.source == "backfill_classified" and .confidence == "classified"'`). Add an AC asserting `is_authoritative('backfill_classified')` returns False for these snapshots.

findings.finding: The plan asserts `scc` produces a `complexity` metric, but the existing codebase integration shows scc's `--format json` output contains per-file `Code` (lines of code) counts — not cyclomatic complexity. Cyclomatic complexity is lizard's domain. Using scc as the complexity source is a capability gap: the plan's `complexity` key cannot be derived from scc's output without additional logic or a switch to lizard's output format.
findings.location: Files section / Approach section: 'run scc/lizard (complexity)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'scc/lizard (complexity)' with two separate tool invocations: `scc --format json <path>` for LOC-based size, and `lizard -o json <path>` (verify flag via `lizard --help`) for cyclomatic complexity. Define which field from each output maps to the `complexity` snapshot key.

findings.finding: `jscpd` is invoked in the repo exclusively via `npx jscpd` (requiring Node.js/npm), but the plan names it as a bare `jscpd` CLI with no acknowledgment of the npx/Node.js runtime dependency. Additionally, jscpd's `--reporters json` output is a structured object (a `duplicates` array with clone instances), not a bare integer — the plan asserts a parseable `clone_count` key but provides no specification of how that count is derived from jscpd's actual JSON schema.
findings.location: Files section: 'jscpd (clones)'; Approach section: 'run jscpd (clones)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Files/Approach section, specify: invocation is `npx jscpd <path> --reporters json --output <tmpdir>`, and `clone_count` is derived as `len(output['statistics']['clones'])` or equivalent field (verify against `jscpd --help` and a sample run). Add Node.js as an explicit runtime precondition.

findings.finding: `jscpd` is asserted as a runnable, parseable clone-detection tool, but it has zero presence in the repository — no wrapper, no output parser, no config, and no prior reference — making the claim that the backfill script can invoke and parse `jscpd` output an unbacked assertion.
findings.location: Approach section: 'run `scc`/`lizard` (complexity) and `jscpd` (clones) over the tree'; Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan's Approach section: 'Invoke `jscpd --reporters json <path>` and extract `statistics.clones.percentage` (or the relevant field) as `clone_count`; document the exact field path so the stub in the test mirrors real output structure.' Also note that unlike scc/lizard (which have parsing infrastructure in engine_b_backends.py), jscpd parsing must be written from scratch.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `jscpd` produces a `clone_count` metric parseable by the backfill script, but neither the invocation flags, output format, nor the extraction logic are specified or verified. The only existing jscpd usage in the repo (`docs/maintenance-audit-runbook.md`, `examples/agent-skills/rebar-janitor/references/patterns.md`) is a manual shell command with specific flags (`--min-tokens 50 --min-lines 8 --reporters json --output <dir>`) and jscpd is explicitly run via `npx` — not as a stable Python-callable binary. The JSON schema of jscpd's output that yields a single `clone_count` integer is not established in the codebase.
findings.location: Files section: 'jscpd (clones)'; Approach section: 'run scc/lizard (complexity) and jscpd (clones)'

findings.finding: The plan references jscpd as the clone-detection tool and specifies it must produce a `clone_count` key, but jscpd has zero presence in the repository — no wrapper, no output parser, no config file, and no documented output format. The parsing protocol (which jscpd JSON field maps to `clone_count`, how to invoke it) is entirely unspecified and cannot be inferred from any existing codebase artifact.
findings.location: Files section ('jscpd (clones)'); Approach section ('run `scc`/`lizard` (complexity) and `jscpd` (clones)')

findings.finding: The plan is silent on idempotency: if the backfill script is run twice against the same store, it will write duplicate snapshots for the same commits, potentially corrupting the trend series. An executor must invent a design choice (skip-if-exists, overwrite, or error) with no guidance.
findings.location: Approach section / Files section: `scripts/backfill_complexity_clone_snapshots.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'Before writing, check whether a snapshot tagged with the commit SHA already exists in the store; if so, skip and log — making the script idempotent on re-run.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
